food_subgroup: Hot milk tea — black tea brewed strong with sweetened condensed milk, served with characteristic frothy head from the pulling technique

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn-specific term exists for teh tarik. Closest parent would be a generic milk tea or tea-with-milk concept. Null retained pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea, black, brewed with milk (closest parent match; teh tarik is a culturally specific preparation not individually coded in FoodEx2)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A045A is the closest defensible parent. Teh tarik's condensed-milk formulation and pulling technique are not individually represented in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for teh tarik identified. Null retained.

preparation_method: pulling (repeated pouring between containers from height to aerate and froth)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard kopitiam or mamak stall serving of teh tarik is approximately 300ml (300g), delivering roughly 186 kcal, 33g carbohydrate, and approximately 28g free sugar predominantly from sweetened condensed milk.

nutrient_source.title: Teh Tarik — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists teh tarik as a named entry. Per-100g values sourced directly from this record. HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 (Atkinson et al.) — milk tea / sweetened condensed milk tea analogous estimate; GI ~55–60 for condensed-milk-sweetened tea beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (American Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for teh tarik exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened condensed milk tea entries (GI range 55–60) from the Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Available carbohydrate per 300g serving = ~33g (11g/100g × 3); GL = (57 × 33) / 100 = 18.8, rounded to 16 in the input based on ~28g sugar as available carbohydrate. Input GL of 16 retained as it reflects the sugar-dominant available carbohydrate fraction (~28g) consistent with HPB source data. Fat and protein from condensed milk moderately attenuate the glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~150mg per 300ml cup) from condensed milk; modest saturated fat (~2.7g per cup); moderate caffeine (~60mg per cup)
hypertension.guidance: Teh tarik contributes approximately 150mg sodium per standard 300ml cup, which is low relative to daily sodium limits; however, habitual consumption of 2–3 cups daily adds ~300–450mg sodium and ~5–8g saturated fat, warranting moderation in hypertensive patients with concurrent cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar load (~28g per 300ml cup from sweetened condensed milk); GL ~16 per cup; a single cup exceeds the WHO daily free sugar recommendation of 25g for women
type2_diabetes.guidance: Type 2 diabetes patients should be urgently counselled to switch from teh tarik to teh-o-kosong (plain black tea, no sugar), eliminating ~28g free sugar and ~183 kcal per cup; for patients unable to eliminate condensed milk immediately, a graded reduction strategy (teh tarik → teh-c siew dai → teh-o kosong) is clinically recommended to reduce glycaemic burden progressively.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.7g per cup) and free sugar (~28g per cup) from condensed milk contribute to dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation risk at habitual 2–3 cups/day intake
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Habitual teh tarik consumption at 2–3 cups daily delivers 5–8g saturated fat and 56–84g free sugar, which may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels; cardiovascular disease patients should limit intake to one cup or fewer per day and consider switching to unsweetened black tea to preserve the cardioprotective theaflavin and catechin benefits of black tea without the lipogenic sugar load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High free sugar (~28g per cup) promotes advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation and systemic pro-inflammatory signalling, worsening RA inflammatory burden
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should strongly consider switching to teh-o-kosong (plain black tea without sugar) to access black tea's anti-inflammatory theaflavin and catechin benefits without the sugar-driven pro-inflammatory AGE load that exacerbates RA disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teh Tarik (literally 'pulled tea') is the iconic hot milk tea of Malaysia and Singapore, widely served at mamak stalls and kopitiams throughout the region. It is prepared by brewing a strong infusion of Ceylon or Assam black tea leaves, then combining the brew with sweetened condensed milk and sometimes evaporated milk. The drink's signature thick frothy head is created by the 'pulling' technique — repeatedly pouring the hot tea mixture between two large containers from a height of 30–60cm. This aerates the liquid, creates a creamy foam, blends the tea and milk thoroughly, and cools the drink to an ideal serving temperature. The result is a rich, sweet, intensely flavoured milk tea with a smooth, velvety texture and a characteristic caramel-like sweetness from the condensed milk. Per 300ml standard cup: approximately 186 kcal, 1.5g fat, 4.5g protein, 33g carbohydrate, ~28g free sugar, 150mg sodium, 171mg calcium, and ~60mg caffeine. GI ~57 (medium), GL ~16 per cup — a HIGH glycaemic load. A single cup of teh tarik exceeds the WHO daily free sugar recommendation of 25g for women. Habitual consumption of 2–3 cups daily is epidemiologically associated with elevated T2DM risk in Singapore's Malay and Indian communities. Clinical priority: T2DM and RA patients should be counselled to switch to teh-o-kosong (plain black tea, no sugar) to eliminate the sugar burden while retaining black tea's anti-inflammatory theaflavin benefits. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo (exact match). Confidence: high.
common_user_queries: how much sugar in teh tarik
teh tarik vs teh o kosong

confidence_reason: Exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Per-100g values sourced directly from the HPB record. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed international GI tables (Atkinson et al. 2021) as no direct teh tarik GI measurement exists; this is clearly documented.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; teh tarik is a liquid beverage with no fibrous plant material remaining after brewing and straining. Thiamin_b1 and vitamin_b6 are 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source for this beverage formulation. Vitamin_d and vitamin_e are 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; condensed milk in Singapore commercial formulations does not contribute meaningful vitamin D or E. Trans_fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source. Monounsaturated_fat_g is -1.0, polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1.0, and water_g is -1.0 because these specific nutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for Teh Tarik. Multiple portions exist in the input (220g, 250g, 300g); the 300ml/300g serving is adopted as the canonical standard kopitiam/mamak serving for GL calculation. The input had four portions all marked is_default=true, which is inconsistent; only the 300g serving is designated default here. GI source is analogous estimate — no direct teh tarik GI measurement found in the University of Sydney GI Database; estimate of 57 is defensible based on sweetened condensed milk tea analogues. Copper (0.02mg/100g), manganese (0.15mg/100g), and vitamin_k (0.1mcg/100g) were sourced from enriched_json in the input with confidence 0.9; these are retained as supporting micronutrient data but not included in the RESULT per_100g template fields as they fall outside the standard template field set.
